components.role: Voltage-controlled attenuator — JFET in ohmic region modulates signal amplitude under LFO control
LFO Schmitt trigger — generates square wave for LFO oscillation
LFO integrator — converts square to triangle for smooth amplitude modulation
Rate control — sets tremolo speed from slow pulse to fast warble
Depth control — sets the intensity of amplitude modulation from subtle to full chop
LFO integrator capacitor — timing element for oscillation
Signal input resistor — forms voltage divider with JFET Rds for amplitude modulation
LFO-to-JFET bias scaling — maps LFO voltage range to JFET gate control range
LFO smoothing cap — rounds triangle edges for softer modulation

dsp_model.description: LFO-driven amplitude modulation; LFO waveform modulates a variable gain element between unity and full attenuation

behavioral_description: A JFET-based tremolo that rhythmically varies the signal amplitude under LFO control. The JFET operates as a voltage-controlled resistor, forming a variable attenuator that the LFO sweeps between full signal pass-through and near-complete attenuation. With a triangle LFO waveform, the modulation is smooth and sinusoidal-like, producing a gentle pulsing. With a square wave LFO, the effect becomes a hard chop — signal fully on, then fully off — for a more dramatic, staccato effect. The rate control sweeps from slow, hypnotic throb (0.5Hz) to fast, Leslie-like warble (15Hz). The depth control sets how much the volume dips — subtle depth (20-30%) adds gentle movement and life, while maximum depth (100%) produces full kill-volume pulsing. The JFET attenuator adds a slight warmth and second-harmonic coloration to the modulated signal. This is the classic tremolo topology found in many boutique pedals and vintage amplifiers.
